title: What is Test-Time Augmentation?
concept: Test-Time Augmentation (TTA) applies data augmentation during inference (testing). By generating multiple augmented variations of a test image, running them through the model, and averaging the predictions, TTA boosts accuracy and confidence reliability.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is a key parameter in the 'Reduce on Plateau' scheduler?

options.text: Patience (how many epochs to wait for improvement before decaying)
Kernel size of the convolutional layers
The resolution of the input images

exercises.explain: Yes! Patience determines the cooldown before a rate decay is triggered.

cards.title: Second Opinions
cards.body: When a doctor looks at a tricky X-ray, they might tilt the scan, zoom in, or adjust the lighting to be absolutely sure of their diagnosis. In deep learning, we usually pass a test image through the model once. If the crop is slightly off, the model might fail. Why not let the model get a 'second opinion'?

cards.title: Test-Time Augmentation
cards.body: Test-Time Augmentation (TTA) is an inference-time technique. Instead of predicting on a single raw test image, we generate multiple augmented versions of it (e.g. flipped, cropped, tilted), run them all through the model, and **average** the output probabilities.

cards.title: A panel of judges
cards.body: Averaging one model prediction is like asking a single judge. Test-Time Augmentation is like forming a panel of judges, but they are all the same person viewing the object from different angles. One judge sees it flipped, another tilted, another zoomed. By voting, they reach a far safer consensus.

cards.title: Cat Inference TTA
cards.body:
A user uploads a cat photo. Under TTA, we generate 4 variations:
1. Original image.
2. Horizontally flipped image.
3. Slightly zoomed-in image.
4. Slightly tilted image.
The model predicts on all 4, and we average their confidence scores for the final answer.

cards.title: It's only for inference
cards.body: Don't confuse TTA with regular data augmentation. Regular augmentation happens **during training** to update weights. TTA happens **during deployment/testing** on frozen weights. It does not update weights; it simply helps the frozen model make a smarter final decision.

cards.title: Single-Pass vs. TTA Inference
cards.body: Comparing the two prediction paths at deployment:

left.text:
1 input -> 1 model pass
Fastest execution (1x)
Vulnerable to bad crops/angles

right.text:
N inputs -> N model passes
Slower execution (Nx)
Higher accuracy & stable confidence

exercises.prompt: What is the core mechanism of Test-Time Augmentation (TTA)?

options.text: Predicting on multiple augmented variations of a test image at inference, then averaging the output probabilities
Updating the weights of the convolutional filters using test labels
Randomly deleting validation folders on disk to save space
Converting the test image to a vector shape

exercises.explain: Correct! TTA averages predictions over multiple augmented versions of the test image.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Test-Time Augmentation updates the model's weights during inference.
exercises.explain: False. TTA runs on a frozen, already-trained model; it does not change any weights.

exercises.sentence: TTA increases final accuracy but requires ___ computation because the model must perform multiple forward passes.
exercises.explain: Since the model runs once per augmented version, TTA multiplies the computational cost at inference.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a major tradeoff when deciding to enable TTA in production?

options.text: Higher prediction accuracy vs. increased latency and computational cost
Smaller model size vs. longer training times
Fewer channels vs. more activation layers

exercises.explain: Correct! TTA boosts accuracy but multiplies latency, which can be a bottleneck for real-time systems.

exercises.prompt: You are deploying a model on a low-power smartwatch that needs to predict in under 10 milliseconds. Should you use TTA?

options.text: No — TTA requires multiple forward passes, which will increase latency and drain battery, making it unsuitable for real-time edge devices
Yes — TTA is mandatory for all watch models to keep the screens on
Yes — because TTA actually speeds up model execution on watch processors
No — because watch screens cannot render augmented color spaces

exercises.explain: Exactly! Latency makes TTA bad for real-time edge devices. Next: how to choose safe TTA transforms!
